title: The trigger of being asked to make a decision under pressure
author: Kalesh

metaDescription:
The question comes and your mind goes blank 

 Someone asks you something simple. What do you want for dinner? Which project should we prioritize? Do you...
htmlContent:
The question comes and your mind goes blank

Someone asks you something simple. What do you want for dinner? Which project should we prioritize? Do you want to stay or go? And suddenly, your brain feels like a computer that just blue-screened. You can't access any information. You can't form a preference. You might even feel your chest tighten, your throat close, or your stomach drop. And the other person is standing there, waiting. Tick tock. The pressure builds.

I've been there. I've stood in a grocery store aisle, phone in hand, while my partner waited for me to choose a pasta sauce. And I couldn't. I literally could not decide. I felt shame rising, panic flickering, and this voice inside saying, "You're an adult. Just pick one. What's wrong with you?"

Here's the thing. That blank-out response isn't a character flaw. It's not you being difficult or indecisive. It's your nervous system doing exactly what it evolved to do. It's protecting you. Stay with me here.

Stephen Porges, the neuroscientist who developed Polyvagal Theory, would explain this as your ventral vagal system shutting down. When you're asked to make a decision under pressure, your nervous system scans for safety. And if it detects even a hint of threat, it shifts into survival mode. Your prefrontal cortex, the part of your brain that weighs options and makes choices, goes offline. You're left with your brainstem and your limbic system. Great for running from tigers. Terrible for picking a salad dressing.

For those who grew up in chaotic, unpredictable, or controlling environments, this response gets wired in early. If making the "wrong" choice as a child meant punishment, criticism, or emotional abandonment, your nervous system learned that decisions are dangerous. It learned that the safest answer is no answer at all. Or that the only acceptable answer is the one the other person wants to hear. So you freeze. You fawn. You dissociate. You say, "I don't know, whatever you want is fine."

And then you walk away feeling invisible. Again.

The hidden cost of freezing in decision moments

Here's what nobody tells you about this trigger. It's not just about the moment itself. It's about what happens after. You feel ashamed. You feel weak. You tell yourself stories about how you're broken, how you'll never get it together, how everyone else seems to handle life so easily. And that shame reinforces the same neural pathways that caused the freeze in the first place. It becomes a loop.

Peter Levine, the founder of Somatic Experiencing, talks about how trauma lives in the body. It's not just a memory in your head. It's a sensation in your chest, a tension in your shoulders, a hollow feeling in your gut. When you're asked to make a decision under pressure, your body remembers. It remembers every time you were punished for choosing wrong. Every time your opinion was dismissed. Every time your needs didn't matter. And it responds the only way it knows how. It shuts you down to keep you safe.

I've worked with clients who can't order food at a restaurant without their heart racing. I've worked with people who freeze when their boss asks for their input in a meeting. I've been that person myself, nodding along while my insides were screaming, "I have no idea what I think about any of this."

Think about it. How many decisions have you avoided today? What did you eat for breakfast? What did you wear? Did you actually choose those things, or did you just default to whatever was easiest, whatever required the least thought, whatever wouldn't draw attention?

The cost of this avoidance is enormous. You lose your sense of self. You lose your voice. You stop knowing what you actually want, because you've been overriding your preferences for so long that they've gone silent. You end up in jobs you don't like, relationships that don't fit, and a life that feels like it belongs to someone else.

And the worst part? You might not even realize it's happening. You just feel tired. Numb. Disconnected. You wonder why life feels so heavy, why you feel so empty, why you can't seem to care about things the way other people do.

Why your nervous system hijacks your choices

Let's get specific about what's happening in your body when you're asked to decide under pressure. Because understanding the mechanics can actually help you interrupt the pattern. Knowledge gives you a foothold. It gives you a place to start.

Your autonomic nervous system has three main states, according to Porges. The ventral vagal state is your social engagement system. It's where you feel safe, connected, and able to think clearly. The sympathetic state is your fight-or-flight system. It's where you feel activated, anxious, or driven. The dorsal vagal state is your freeze-collapse system. It's where you shut down, dissociate, or feel numb.

When someone asks you to make a decision under pressure, your nervous system does a quick scan. If it detects safety, you stay in ventral vagal. You can access your prefrontal cortex. You can weigh options, consider consequences, and make a choice that aligns with your values. Easy.

But if your nervous system detects threat, and for many of us, pressure feels like threat, it drops you into sympathetic or dorsal vagal. If you go sympathetic, you might make a rushed, impulsive decision just to end the discomfort. You say yes to something you don't want. You agree to a plan you'll regret. You pick the first option that comes to mind because the anxiety of not deciding is worse than making a bad choice.

If you go dorsal, you freeze. Your mind goes blank. You can't access your preferences because your prefrontal cortex has gone offline. You might feel heavy, slow, or disconnected from your own body. You might say "I don't know" on repeat. You might just stare at the person, unable to form words.

Both responses are survival strategies. Neither one is a choice. And neither one helps you live the life you actually want.

For those looking for gentle nervous system support, something worth trying is NOW L-Theanine 200mg (paid link). It's an amino acid that can help take the edge off that anxious activation without making you drowsy. It's not a cure, but it can create enough space in your nervous system to start practicing new responses.

How to start reclaiming your decision-making ability

I want to be honest with you. There's no quick fix for this. You can't just "decide to be more decisive" if your nervous system is in survival mode. That's like telling someone with a broken leg to just walk it off. It doesn't work that way. But you can start small. You can start safe. And you can build from there.

The first step is to recognize when you're being triggered. Pay attention to the physical sensations. Does your chest tighten? Do your shoulders go up? Does your breathing get shallow? Do you feel a hollow space opening in your stomach? Notice these signals without judging them. Just notice. That's already a form of agency. You're observing your nervous system instead of being run by it.

The second step is to slow down. You don't have to answer immediately. You can say, "Let me think about that for a moment." You can say, "I'll get back to you on that." You can take a deep breath and feel your feet on the floor. You can orient to the room, looking around and noticing where you are. These are Polyvagal-informed techniques that help your nervous system return to ventral vagal. They give your prefrontal cortex a chance to come back online.

One supplement that many people find calming for their nervous system is Doctors Best Magnesium Glycinate (paid link). Magnesium is involved in over 300 biochemical reactions in your body, and it plays a direct role in regulating your nervous system. Many trauma survivors are deficient. Adding it in can sometimes make a noticeable difference in your baseline stress levels.

The third step is to practice making low-stakes decisions. Start with things that don't matter much. What color socks do you want to wear today? Do you want your coffee black or with milk? Do you want to walk left or right when you leave your house? These tiny choices might feel silly, but they're neural exercises. Each time you make a choice, even a small one, you're building the pathway that says, "I have preferences. My preferences matter. It's safe to choose."

A structured workbook that many C-PTSD survivors find practical is The Complex PTSD Workbook (paid link). It walks you through exercises that help you reconnect with your own needs and desires, which is essential for rebuilding your decision-making capacity. It's not a substitute for therapy, but it's a solid tool for doing the work between sessions.

The fourth step is to get curious about the cost of not deciding. What are you giving up when you let others choose for you? What parts of yourself are you silencing? What would it feel like to actually want something and say so? This is scary work. It brings up old grief. It brings up anger. It brings up the realization that you've been abandoning yourself for a long time.

For understanding emotional neglect specifically, a helpful resource is Running on Empty (paid link). It's about childhood emotional neglect, which is often the root of this inability to know or voice what you want. When your emotions and needs were ignored as a child, you learned to ignore them too. This book helps you see that pattern clearly and start changing it.

What recovery actually looks like

I'm not going to tell you that you'll wake up one day and suddenly feel confident making decisions under pressure. That's not how this works. Recovery is slower than that. It's more like learning to walk after a long illness. You take a step. You wobble. You take another step. You fall. You get back up. And gradually, your legs remember how to hold you.

There will be days when you still freeze. There will be moments when the old shame rises up and tells you you're broken. That's okay. That's not failure. That's your nervous system doing what it's been trained to do. Your job isn't to never get triggered. Your job is to notice it, to stay present with it, and to gently guide yourself back to safety. Again and again. Without judgment. Without the whip of self-criticism.

One thing that helped me was learning to say, "I need a minute to think about that." Just those words. They give me time. They give my nervous system a chance to settle. And they communicate to the other person that I'm taking the question seriously. Most people are fine with that. The ones who aren't, well, that's information too.

Another thing that helped was working with a therapist who understands trauma. If you have access to that kind of support, I highly recommend it. Janina Fisher's work on structural dissociation is incredibly helpful for understanding why parts of you freeze while other parts of you watch in frustration. Richard Schwartz's Internal Family Systems model offers a compassionate way to work with the parts that are afraid to choose.

And here's the hardest truth I've had to accept. Reclaiming your ability to decide means accepting that you will sometimes choose wrong. You will make decisions that don't work out. You will disappoint people. You will disappoint yourself. That's part of being human. It's not a sign that you should stop choosing. It's a sign that you're alive, that you're trying, that you're willing to risk being seen.

Sit with that. What would it mean for you to be someone who makes choices, even imperfect ones? What would it mean to let others see what you actually want? What would it mean to trust that you can handle the consequences of your own decisions?

I don't have neat answers. I have more questions. But I know this much. The trigger of being asked to make a decision under pressure is real. It's painful. And it's not permanent. Your nervous system can learn new patterns. Your brain can build new pathways. Your voice can come back. It might take time. It might take tears. But it's possible.

Start with one small choice today. Just one. See what happens. Notice how it feels. And then maybe, tomorrow, try another one. That's all any of us can do.
